speaker: interviewee
text: I think only one child or two children. And he was only about five years in Cleveland, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Moshe Nisenbaum. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 19th, 1996. We're with Cela Sarna. This is tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my-- This is my brother. His name was Yankel Nisenbaum. And he died in Majdanek, with me, when I was in Majdanek

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was taken in Miedzyrzecz. And he send it to mine aunt, to Cleveland. And that's how I got it. This is mine sister, mine oldest sister, and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leah and Shmuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their-- do you remember their last name? You don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I tell you something? That just this minute, I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You can come back to it. When was this? Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I think, after their wedding. And they send it over to mine aunt. And when I was there after the war, she gave me. It was a old picture and very wrinkled. I think they made it look nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they married? Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were married in Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Greenbaum. Oh, gee.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Yankel Nisenbaum, and his wife, [NON-ENGLISH] Nisenbaum, and this little boy, Yitzhak Nisenbaum. And they were on vacation a year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [NON-ENGLISH], not far. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] This is my sister, Gitel. And this is my brother, Yankel. They made a picture before the war, in Miedzyrzecz. And they send it to mine aunt. All those pictures I got from mine aunt in Cleveland. Because we didn't see anybody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, this picture was taken in the Falk School, Falk schule, when we were maybe five or six years old. I think I was five. Maybe she was six. I am the-- I'm the bottom, the second from the right side. She is on the top, the fourth-- the fourth girl--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all the girl-- from all the children, the teachers, we are the only survivors. I got this picture when I was in Australia, and I met her. And she was in Russia. And that's how she had the picture. And she made me a duplicate and send me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you tell us again which one she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the fourth from the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fourth from the right, on the top row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haycha. But I don't remember her second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because now she remarried. And-- and I don't know her name either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was around 1930?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was around-- yes. Yes, maybe earlier. No, in '29. I was only five years old in this picture. That was the first year in school. That's what they take it. And then later, when I went into public school, I was seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Are you going to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is our wedding day picture. This is me, Cela Sarna, and my husband, Jack Sarna. It was a beautiful wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany, was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. The dress we borrowed it from a place. [LAUGHS] We paid, I think, $2 for it. And the-- the veil was made-- the doctor gave me gauze in the hospital. So I made the-- I made the veil.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was January the-- the 20th, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Be quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [NON-ENGLISH] Gitter, my brother-in-law. And his son, Max Gitter. This was his bar mitzvah. And my sister, Paula Gitter, and-- and Max-- Martin, on the bottom. The last one was Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in the day of their bar mitzvah. And I don't know exactly when it was. I wasn't at the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in-- in New York City. This is mine sister, Itkie, with her little girl, Hava. This was made in Russia, or on the way, when she came from Russia. But anyway, this was made in '45. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Germany, we came to Deggendorf. They only gave us wool. And we had nothing to do with the wool.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided I'm going to-- the wool was very heavy. I'll make sweaters for everybody. Because whoever was left after the war, we were like one big happy family. I don't know how happy, but one family. This is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one? The first on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the right, yeah. To me. This was his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second on the right is his brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]. And he is very sick now. And this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Next on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Moshe-- I can't see very well, who they are. [LAUGHS] No, I cannot identify them too well. So I better not do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. And this is my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one, two, three, four--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the-- two-- two girls on the bottom. This is mine cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- her name is Hubert, Sally Hubert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're the girl on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's in Australia. And I'm the girl on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is Cela Sarna. My husband is right in the back of me. He holds me around, with his hand. This is Charlie, I can see. Mine--

speaker: interviewer
text: The first on the left is Charlie?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, yeah. This is-- and this is Morris. This is my husband's two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris is next to him, on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest, I cannot even see it. So we'll be good without it. But I know them all. We're meeting-- we are meeting very often. By the Jewish Society. We still have a society. This is me, Cela Sarna, and the baby, Shirley Sarna. She was just three months old in there. In Deggendorf, Germany--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done in a country club in Westchester. There was my-- mine son-in-law's parents' 50th anniversary. In-- on the right side is my grandson, Alec, and my son-in-law, Stephen. My daughter, Shirley, and Adam, the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year. It was taken in fall, in '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much for sharing these pictures with us.
